speaker: interviewer
text: You should be immune already, you know. He knows much, much more than I'm saying here.

speaker: interviewee
text: You told me that they arrested some of the children. Oh, me too--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so if you can pick up from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because now when I'm in the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, camera is set, all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three with Freda Rosenblatt. Freda, can you tell us a little bit about activities in the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the activities, we were children in the city, about six boys and six girls. No supervision, no food. In order to get some food, the boys every month in my hometown used to be at the bazaar, the market. Used to be at the villages. So they used to go steal some food. They used to call it the day when you're not hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they wouldn't come-- wouldn't go to school, but gone to market to steal some food. Nobody would dare to squeal, may God help us, because the teachers in school used to ask, where are the children every Monday? Why aren't the boys in school? Nobody would dare to say, because for them, it was nothing. They would take a knife and kill you. It was-- it was not a normal time. We'd say, oh, we don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky because I was living in [NON-ENGLISH]. And some people used to give me some food. And I used to bring it and share it with the rest of the girls. But it was cold. It was winter. And they didn't add any wood to heat up our place. So from so many broken Jewish houses, we used to go in the night, maybe to break another piece of wood to keep us warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this was illegal, because those houses became the property of the Soviet government. And who's going to touch those houses? This is like a criminal. So in the night we used to go to tear up pieces of wood. I remember one night, the police were chasing us. And we started to run, practically barefoot, took off our boots or galoshes and run in the snow, practically in socks. And when we came running to the place where we were living, and with the clothes, on the beds, under the blankets, whatever we had. And the police came after us, took us all out of the bed. We're all dressed up. And said, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the pieces of wood were under the bed, whatever we could manage steal to hide. So they took our names. And the next day, they came to school and arrested us all, called us in the office. And the whole school didn't know what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were the children, boys and girls from the orphanage, arrested? And where we arrested, were taken is my parents' home was the KGB. Then I was brought in as a common criminal because we tried to-- a little bit of wood.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in and my home-- remember, this was the place where I was living a normal life as a child with my parents, with my brother. And this I was brought in as a criminal. And we were warned, once more, you're caught, we send you to prison. Because in Russia, age 14, they can put you in prison. Somehow they got in touch with the orphanage, and they send us a kind of canteen, something to give us something to eat. But we were warned, if we won't be good children, make good marks, no food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the house look like inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the same-- sure, it wasn't the same. There are offices, decay. But for me, it was something, such a terrible experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my home. This was my father built just before the war. I don't know if there  is words to describe my feelings. What have I done?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was hungry. I was cold. So we broke a few pieces of wood from a broken home. It's not like we killed somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: But for the Russians, this doesn't mean anything to them. To bring children from an orphanage without food together, with no supervision together-- they used to bring once in a month a little bit, I remember, dry peas and dry beans, something. Where are we going to cook with no dishes? There's no kitchen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought bread for a month. We finished it in two, three days we were so hungry. I remember they brought some eggs. We ate them raw, because nowhere to cook them. This was our life. The orphanage was in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember once I went to relatives, friends from the family. And they give me a big fresh bread, and a cup with-- with own home jam. And I brought it. And they told me, they told-- Freda, take it home. And you will have it for a few days to eat. I thought, if I have it a few days to eat, I won't share it with the rest of the-- rest of the girls. Not fair. I will eat. What about the rest of the children? So I remember I work them up. We sat down on the bed. And we finished the old bread with  the old jam. Next there was nothing to eat. We were hungry, but all of us were hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the life. We shared everything. Our clothes, our food, our pain, our misery. We were singing songs related to the miserable life we were living. This was already after the war, because this was after the war, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is beginning of '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already end of '45, beginning of '46, because I went to the orphanage December '45. And this was January, February of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that you had some family in the village. Can you clarify that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This wasn't a village. This was a town.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the town, I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even know how we were related, but God meant to help me. Those people's name were Gold, Uncle Yakov and Auntie Rahel-- their name of family Gold, but those people were really pure gold. One day, Uncle Yakov, he came to the orphanage and told me, Freda, come. He took me, was-- that's what I had on my body, clothes. They took me in their home. My aunt was at that time pregnant with a youngest son, which now in Israel. I mean, the aunt is gone, already the uncle, unfortunate. But their youngest son, [NON-ENGLISH], she was pregnant with him. And the other, the middle son, was about 10 years old or so. One little boy did lost in Transnistria when he was a newborn child. And the older son managed to go away for the children from Transnistria. And he was in '46, he was already to-- in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those people took me into their home. And at that time, from Tschernowitz, people were allowed to go to Romania, because part of Bukovina belonged to Romania. So people bought false passports, false papers that they are from Romania, and they were taken to Transnistria from Romania by the Romanians, but they actually are from Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: It cost money, was paid with gold. Those people endangered their lives to take out, first of all, a child from a Russian orphanage under false pretense, under false name, to pay with gold, to take me to Romania. Had they been caught, oh, they would-- they would be killed or put for life in prison. But they endangered their lives and took me to Romania. This already was April '46. When we came to Romania, all of Romania was orphans. Already teenagers, children, and they have orphanages. And at that time, Israel wasn't any country. But the Zionist organization were in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they started to go with boats illegally to Cyprus, because the English was still in Israel. It was Palestine. And they wouldn't allow. It wasn't enough to be for so many under the barbed wire. Now the Jews got taken to Cyprus, again under barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of Romania was with orphan, with orphanages. Not one single country offered to adopt Jewish children. I remember a friend of mine, she had a grandmother in the States. She wanted to take her out. The United States wouldn't let her in. Jewish children-- there was no place for the Jewish children, where to be and where to run. We were practically-- Even in Romania, was under a rabbi, there where I got actually my Jewish education. He, too, endangered his life, keeping children, bringing them from Bessarabia, from Tschernowitz, and keeping them, about 200 children. A lot of the children were still wetting their bed, crying, nightmares. The younger children to sleep with their lights on, because they were afraid from the darkness. I was then time 14 years old, and I took care already on smaller children, helping them to wash and to take care on themselves, like children taking care on children. This was still 1949. When Israel became a state and because Israel didn't became a communist country, now the Romanians wouldn't let out the Jews to Israel. So the young, the orphanages got completely make illegal, that they were Zionist. So the rabbi was arrested, and we were taking over from the Romanian-- wards of the Romanian government.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was arrested because as far as they're concerned, he was pushing illegally the religion, because the communist religion is there is no God, there's no religion. So they arrested him, and arrested his son, too. And us children were taken over from the-- from the state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what he told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What the rabbi taught? Taught us religion. He taught us to be good, to believe in God, because it was strictly orthodox. Some of the older girls already married. He married them off-- but already by 17, 18 years old. As the matter of fact, here in Toronto-- one of the rabbi's-- she's already a great-grandmother now. She's a-- her husband passed away. She's a rabbi's-- her husband was a rabbi. She lives here in Toronto. The rabbi, himself, passed away in the States. After he was arrested, it wasn't [INAUDIBLE] from the United States, from all over the world to lay out this grand rabbi, because what-- humanitarian grounds. He came to the States. He still was doing a lot of good work. And he passed away. And when he-- his son took over. He told his son, my son, I don't have any money to leave you. One thing I'm going to leave you, take care of poor children. This is-- and he's still doing-- his son is already a grandfather-- great-grandfather, I don't know. But this was the work from this rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he taught you to speak Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew, actually learned in Israel. He taught us the prayer. When I go to the synagogue to payer, I know everything what I read, because it was really very orthodox. To read Yiddish and the prayers, everything Jewish holiday, because I really didn't have the chance as a child to learn all those things. He was the one that taught the children about Judaism and the religion, and not to be ashamed, to be proud to be Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember he used to tell us, 'If you are embarrassed of your Jewish background, how do you want the Nazis to like you? They  will be embarrassed with you, definitely. Be proud of yourself. You didn't do anything wrong.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's exactly how I feel. Don't be embarrassed to be named your Jewish names, or about your Jewish heritage. You didn't commit any crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Rabbi Portugal-- but used to call him a Skulener rebbe-- because the town from where he is was Skulen. So they used to call him the Skulener rebbe. But the thing, what he was telling us was to be proud to be Jews. Because if you're embarrassed with your own self, why shouldn't our enemies feel embarrassed with you, too? And this actually made a big impression on me, personally. So-- So the close of 1949, the Romanians started to let out people from Romania. Was that because of world pressure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be. Also one of the ministers, and a Jewish woman Anna Pauker. All her life she was sitting in the prison because she was a communist. And when Romania become a communist country, she became a minister in Romania. And she started to let out the Jews. She paid with her life. She disappeared. Nobody knows where she disappeared. Because with Stalin, today you're a big shot, tomorrow you're nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that time, they started to let out Jews. I remember to go to get the application, we had to go to the minister of whatever they call it. We had to jump because we're already, in the words of the government, we had to jump to the big fence in the night so they wouldn't know that we're going to apply to go to Israel. So at last, we applied.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every week they started to send out Jews to Israel. I mean, there was the name of the boat, And that boat was place only for 500 people. So there was crowding in 1,000 people, just as long as they let out the Jews. Israel consulate was in Bucharest. And they tried as much as possible-- the young people-- to send out young people. Because-- Because they started to take in the army, the Romania army, so they started to take Jewish people, Jewish boys too. So they wanted to look out the Jewish people, Jewish boys and girls, so they were able to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh boy. It's a lot of noise. So you fell into that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I fell into that group. And we arrived to Israel September 1950. Dear lord, for me to be free. At last, to go to Israel like a dream comes true. Because there were cases, they would take off from the boats-- that you're something, that they wanted to take off from the boat in Romania. You have to go back. You're not allowed to go to Israel. So when I came off the boat, I felt like falling on the ground and kissing the Israeli soil. Because the life in Israel was at that time so difficult and so hard to live. But for me, it was a paradise. People couldn't understand. I was working so hard, and people were thinking, Freda, you're so happy. You're so-- you don't know what I went through in my life. Because my life was a hell. And to be in Israel, to work-- at last nobody had to give me anything, food, because I was working very hard for every piece of bread, I was working, I was earning it. First time I went and bought myself a dress, a pair of shoes, I was so happy, even though I was working very hard for it. I remember I came to Israel, it was exactly Rosh Hashanah. And I was staying in a boarding room, [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was given challah. The first time I saw challah, dear lord. And I can eat so much challah, as much I want. I saw the rest of the girl picked up challah, also took a piece of challah, because I could eat. It was for me a luxury. Then I started to work in a-- in a place were all the-- where all the immigrants came in. At that time it was a camp of 10,000 people, came all about the world. It was 1950s, only two years a state.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Haifa. And I was working in the kitchen, helping with the cooking, with the cleaning, and giving people to eat. And those people had to be kept in those-- we'll call it a quarantine, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: The medical problems.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they had to be kept, yeah, because they were afraid that they would have trachoma and all kinds of diseases-- 10,000 people at a time. So if was very difficult. People just came in from concentration camp, from all kinds of places, from the Middle Eastern countries. There were children, young girls brought in from Morocco with syphilis, all kind of diseases, with trachoma, eye disease is contagious. And they were kept there. And at last they were let out, gone to kibbutzim and to the army. And this I worked for about two years until I learned the Hebrew language. Not in a school. I couldn't afford to go to school. But as a young girl, between young people, sure, I've learned the language. And I started to work in a kindergarten as a teacher's helper. I couldn't afford to go to school to learn teacher or anything, but knowing already the Hebrew language. I could already--

speaker: interviewer
text: Make your way.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet up as a young 18-year-old or 19-year-old with other people from your area, in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, some. Yeah, I was already a teenager. When I came to Israel, I was already a teenager. I was already, you know-- but my life, what I went through, my life wasn't-- you don't forget it. The thing is, we were all children-- all young girls or boys who went through all this from Romania, or from Poland, or from Europe. Because everybody from those countries went through this hell of a life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're sitting and talking and remember it. You don't forget things like this. So for me, Israel was a paradise. And when people were complaining because people were living in tents, because there weren't any place to live. And I was living about five, six girls in one room. For me, everything was fine. I could go. I could come. I could sing. I could dance. It was something, a change. I mean, you don't forget what happened. And then in 1953, I met my husband. What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo Rosenblatt. And in 1954, February 22, '54, it's actually next month is going to be 41 years since we were married. And our son Henry-- actually his Hebrew name is Elhanan-- he's named after my father. He was born in March 8, '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, that's right. And in '57, we moved to Australia because my husband's family-- my husband sister was living in Canada, and his parents wanted to join their daughter. So they all decided to go to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, somehow Canada didn't let in people from Israel. Why? Had to do with a quota or something, I don't know. So we went to Australia because there were relatives in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were told because Australia and Canada is the Commonwealth-- so we went to Australia. And after two years, we came to Canada. We're living in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Melbourne. And then we came to Canada, to Montreal. And there my two daughter were born in Montreal. My older daughter Sarah, she's named after my mother, and my younger daughter Pnina, she's named after my mother's older sister, who was also killed in Poland, in the concentration camps, or who knows where.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your husband survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband was in Russia. It was luck that his father had a big truck. And when they started to bomb Poland, his father took his family and some more people, and they ran into a forest to escape the bombing. So meanwhile the Germans started to come closer, and they ran to the Russian border to the Ukraine. And they closed the border to the Ukraine. And from there they run. It wasn't an easy run, because his father was taken when 1941, the-- the war started. His father was taken in the Russian army. And his mother, with the three children, were running from place to place, from place to place in Russia. And eventually they met with the father. And they were in [NON-ENGLISH], in Siberia, all over the places. And then after the war, they came to Poland. From Poland they came to Germany, to the-- to the camps, for the refugee camps. And my husband came to Israel in 1948. He came as a volunteer to fight in the army at that time, to independence of Israel. So he was fighting in the mountains of Jerusalem, practically, with bare hands. So-- that's-- that's how we met in '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've in Toronto since when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be-- in June it's going to be 16 years since we live in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children live in Toronto as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- yes, all three children are married. Henry has two sons. His wife, Rebecca, and their two sons, David and Joshua. And my older one, David, is eight and a half years old. When I was deported, I was nine years old, only six months older than my older grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I look at this little boy, he's protected as a child. And the thought that this pure-- this innocent little boy could have been me, and lived at that time, it's so scary that this child-- I hope and pray that those children will never, ever know what me, as a child, ever, ever knew, especially now, denial of the Holocaust, neo-Nazis. What is going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give them, God forbid, an opportunity, they're going to do the same thing to our children, our grandchildren-- what the Hitlerites of yesteryear did to us, and maybe even worse. After all, they have already lessons from those Hitlers and Eichmanns and the-- and all-- and the Himmlers, and the Mengeles. They will do even much more because they have their role models.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how we have to be careful and teach our children, not to turn a blind eye, and not to say that happened 50 years ago. That was told in time for Hitler. But then it was too late.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I have my older daughter Sarah. She's named after my mother, and her husband Rob. My younger daughter Pnina, and her husband Michael. Michael is also a son of a Holocaust survivor from Poland. And as I said, my two grandchildren, David and Joshua. I hope I will have much more grandchildren. And I hope and pray that their future and their lives should be better than mine and the children from my generation. And those people who called themselves the super race, they are not super race. They are super barbarians. And that's how they have to be referred to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Freda. Are you in touch with people in Israel from Transnistria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was a year and a half ago, I was in Israel after 36 years. I couldn't have an opportunity to go until now. And I met my friends, survivors, from Bershad. Also we were together in orphanages. And you know how many times, after so many years, we're sitting and talking and going back to those times, even so many years later. Those things are not forgotten. And God forbid, because to forget the Holocaust, it's as bad as denying it. Because who would imagine that 50 years later, we will be still talking. People will deny it. People will-- look, it happened. We have to fight that it happened. Nobody would ever-- because if somebody would have told me, 50 years ago, that I will be able to talk about this and warn, I would never believe it. Because people will say, Germany will never exist. Nazi will never exist. This will never happen. Look what's happening. Look what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's how much is important for us to still with our survivors. Because I am from the youngest generations of the survivors. I don't know how long I will be living, 10, 20-- I don't know. If I don't talk, who will? Freda, thank you very much for sharing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.
